1: The messenger formula opens a throne-room oracle: Jeremiah is sent to the palace (beit hamelech) itself — the seat of Davidic authority — to confront the reigning king directly. This is the most confrontational prophetic assignment in Jeremiah's ministry up to this point; the setting at the palace gates (v. 2) ensures maximum public exposure.
2: hayoshev al-kise David (hayyōšēb ʿal-kissēʾ dāwid): 'You who sit on the throne of David.' The address establishes Davidic covenant accountability: sitting on David's throne is not merely political office but covenant succession. The king who occupies that throne inherits not only David's authority but David's covenantal responsibilities — the obligations of the shepherd-king (cf. Ezek 34:2-4 where YHWH castigates the same shepherds). Entry through these gates (sha'ar hazeh) marks the subjects as those under the king's jurisdiction and therefore his responsibility.
3: asu mishpat utzedaqah (ʿăśû mišpāṭ ûṣĕdāqāh): 'Do what is just and right.' The mishpat-tzedaqah pair is the prophetic shorthand for covenant righteousness — appearing together in Isa 1:27; 9:7; Amos 5:24; Mic 6:8. Mishpat (justice, the execution of legally right outcomes) and tzedaqah (righteousness, the relational quality of right-standing) together describe both procedural and character-based right living. The three protected categories — ger (foreigner), yatom (orphan), almanah (widow) — are the covenant's permanently vulnerable members, whose protection is a test case for whether covenant justice is real (Exod 22:21-24; Deut 10:18; Ps 146:9).
4: ki im asoh ta'asu et-hadavar hazeh uvau visha'arei habayit hazeh melakim yoshavim ledavid al-kis'o: 'For if you carefully obey this command, then kings will continue to pass through the gates of this palace.' The double infinitive absolute (asoh ta'asu, 'doing you shall do') intensifies the conditionality: if and only if justice is genuinely practiced, the Davidic dynasty continues. The royal continuity is placed entirely on the condition of covenantal obedience — not on military power or political skill. Chariots and horses (rakhuv berekev uvesusim) denote the full ceremonial display of royal power, promised as the reward of obedience.
5: ki im lo tishme'u et-hadvarim ha'elleh bishvi nishba'ti (kî ʾim-lōʾ tišmĕʿû ʾet-haddĕbārîm hāʾēlleh bî nišbaʿtî): 'But if you refuse to listen to these words, I swear by myself, declares the LORD.' Bishvi nishba'ti (I swear by myself) — the divine self-oath, used when no higher authority exists to swear by (cf. Gen 22:16; Isa 45:23; Heb 6:13). YHWH's self-oath is the strongest possible guarantee: the palace will become a ruin (chorba). The same oath form is used for the Abrahamic promises (Gen 22:16) and for eschatological judgment (Isa 45:23) — placing this royal warning in the same register as the founding covenantal promises.
6: ki-koh amar YHWH al-beit melech Yehudah gilad attah li rosh halvanon (kî-kōh ʾāmar Yhwh ʿal-bêt melek yĕhûdāh gilʿād ʾattāh lî rōʾš hallĕbānôn): 'You are Gilead to me, the heights of Lebanon — yet I will surely make you a wasteland.' Gilead and Lebanon were proverbial for lushness, abundant timber, and fertility (cf. 22:23; Mic 7:14; Nah 1:4). The palace built of Lebanese cedar (cf. 1 Kings 7:2-3: 'the palace of the forest of Lebanon') is itself the cedar-landmark. The irony is embedded in the beauty: the very thing that makes the palace magnificent (its cedar construction) is what fuels its destruction in v. 7 — the finest cedar trees felled and thrown into the fire.
7: vehiqaddashti aleikha mashchitim ish uklav (wĕhiqqaddaštî ʿālêkā maśḥîtîm ʾîš ûkĕlāyw): 'I will send destroyers against you, each armed with weapons; they will fell your finest cedar trees and throw them into the fire.' Mashchitim (destroyers) — the same root as the corrupt earth before the flood (Gen 6:12) and the destroying angel at the Exodus (Exod 12:23). YHWH 'consecrates' (hiqdash) these destroyers — using holy-war vocabulary for the enemies. The cedar trees ('your finest', arzel arzicha) being cut and burned inverts the palace's construction: built from Lebanon's cedars (1 Kgs 7), it returns to ash.
8: ve'avru goyim rabbim al-ha'ir hazot ve'amru ish el-re'ehu: 'Many nations will pass by this city, and they will ask one another, 'Why has the LORD done this to such a great city?'' The tourist-horror response from passing nations echoes the covenant curse of Deut 29:24-26 almost verbatim: 'all the nations will ask, 'Why has the LORD done this to this land?'' The question from outsiders (goyim rabbim) is rhetorical — they are not expecting an answer but drawing attention to the theological magnitude of what has happened to a city YHWH specially chose.
9: tachat asher azavu et-brit YHWH eloheihem (taḥat ʾăšer ʿāzĕbû ʾet-bĕrît Yhwh ʾĕlōhêhem): 'Because they abandoned the covenant of the LORD their God.' The answer to v. 8's question is delivered in one clause: covenant abandonment. The word azav (abandoned/forsook) is the strongest verb for willful covenant breach — used for Hagar abandoning Ishmael (Gen 21:14), for children abandoning a parent, and for Israel abandoning YHWH (1 Kgs 18:18; Jer 1:16; 2:13). Serving other gods (vayishttachavu la'elohim acherim) is the specific content of the abandonment.
10: al-tivku lamet veal-tanudu lo (ʾal-tibkû lāmēt wĕʾal-tānūdû lô): 'Do not weep for the dead king or mourn for him; but weep bitterly for the one who goes into exile.' The 'dead king' is Josiah, killed at Megiddo (609 BCE) — the greatest reforming king of Judah's last century. The 'one who goes into exile' is Jehoahaz (Shallum), deposed by Pharaoh Neco after three months and taken to Egypt (2 Kgs 23:31-34). The counter-intuitive reversal: Josiah who died at home is less to be mourned than Jehoahaz who lives in exile; death is less final than exile from the covenant land. This reflects the Deuteronomic theology that life in the covenant land with YHWH is the supreme good.
11: ki-koh amar YHWH el-Shallum ben-Yoshiyahu: 'For this is what the LORD says about Shallum son of Josiah.' The throne name 'Jehoahaz' is replaced with the personal name 'Shallum' (shallum, related to shalom, peace/wholeness) — an ironic use since he will die far from peace. Historical note: Jehoahaz/Shallum reigned only three months before Pharaoh Neco deposed him and installed Jehoiakim in his place, making him the first Davidic king taken captive to a foreign land in Jeremiah's lifetime.
12: ki bimakom asher heggelu oto sham yamut veet-ha'aretz hazot lo-yireh od: 'He will die in the place he has been taken captive, and he will never see this land again.' The death in captivity without return is the reversal of the promised land theology: YHWH's gift to Abraham and his descendants was the specific land of Canaan as their perpetual inheritance. To die in a foreign land, unburied in the covenant soil, is the most complete form of covenant displacement in the ancient Near Eastern mind.
13: hoy boneh beto belo-tzedek vaaliyotav belo mishpat (hôy bōneh bêtô bĕlōʾ-ṣedeq wăʿăliyyōtāw bĕlōʾ mišpāṭ): 'Woe to him who builds his palace through injustice and his upper rooms through unrighteousness.' The hoy (woe) formula opens a formal lament-curse, typically used over the dead (cf. 1 Kgs 13:30: 'woe, my brother!'). Applying it to a living king is a death pronouncement while still alive. The two-part accusation — building without tzedek (justice/right) and without mishpat (justice/procedure) — inverts the demand of v. 3. The specific violation: bere'ehu ya'avod chinam upho'alo lo yitten lo (making his countrymen work for nothing, not paying wages) — a direct violation of Lev 19:13 and Deut 24:14-15.
14: ha'omer evneh-li beit middot vaaliyot mevushot (hāʾōmēr ʾebneh-lî bêt middôt wăʿăliyyôt mĕruwwāḥôt): 'Who says, 'I will build myself a grand palace with large upper rooms.'' The specific features listed — cedar paneling (sapun bearazim) and red paint/ochre (shaoh beshasha) — are the luxury markers of elite construction. Red ochre was expensive; cedar from Lebanon was the prestige building material. The contrast with the modest requirements of Josiah's just reign (vv. 15-16) makes Jehoiakim's palace an explicit monument to self-indulgence at the expense of covenant responsibility.
15: hatimaloch ki attah mitcharer baaretz (hătimloḵ kî ʾattāh mitḥārēr bāʾārez): 'Do you think that making yourself a rival in cedar proves you are a king?' The rhetorical question punctures royal pretension: cedar paneling is not the measure of kingship. The contrast is immediate: avicha (your father, Josiah) 'ate and drank' — lived normally and adequately — 'but also practiced justice and righteousness, and things went well for him.' Asah mishpat utzedaqah (practiced justice and righteousness) repeats v. 3's demand but now as historical example: Josiah actually did it, and it worked. The causal connection between covenant justice and tov lo (things went well for him) is stated flatly as historical fact.
16: dan din-ani veevyon az tov halo hi hada'at oti neum YHWH (dān dîn-ʿānî wĕʾebyôn ʾāz ṭôb hălōʾ-hîʾ haddaʿat ʾōtî nĕʾum Yhwh): 'He defended the cause of the poor and needy; then it went well. Is that not what it means to know me? declares the LORD.' This is one of the most important prophetic equations in the entire OT: da'at YHWH (knowing YHWH) is defined as defending the poor and needy, not as theological knowledge. The question is rhetorical — it demands the answer 'yes.' To know YHWH in the covenantal sense (yada') is not intellectual belief about YHWH but relational fidelity expressed in the concrete treatment of the vulnerable. Hosea establishes the same equation (Hos 4:1-2: 'no faithfulness, no loyalty, no knowledge of God in the land, but cursing, lying, murder, stealing, adultery'); Matthew's parable of the sheep and goats (Matt 25:31-46) is the NT's most direct restatement: 'whatever you did for one of the least of these brothers ... you did for me.'
17: ki eyn eineicha velibcha ki im al-bitscha veal-dam hanaqui (kî ʾên ʿênêkā wĕlibbĕkā kî ʾim-ʿal-biṣʿekā wĕʿal-dam hannāqî): 'But you have no eyes or heart for anything except your own dishonest gain, and for shedding innocent blood.' Betzaʿ (dishonest gain / greed for cut-off profit) is related to the verb 'cut off' — profit extracted by cutting off others' rights. The three parallel sins — dishonest gain (vetza), innocent blood (dam hanaqui), oppression (ashaq), and violence (mirtzah) — are the diagnostic opposite of v. 3's demands. Jehoiakim is the anti-Josiah.
18: lachen koh-amar YHWH el-Yehoyaqim ben-Yoshiyahu: 'Therefore this is what the LORD says about Jehoiakim son of Josiah.' The death lament for Jehoiakim inverts the standard royal mourning. The double-negative structure — 'no one will mourn for him, crying 'Ah, my brother!' ... no one will mourn for him, crying 'Ah, my lord!'' — lists the two standard forms of kinship lament (sibling lament and subject lament) and denies both. Hoy achi / hoy achot (ah, my brother / ah, my sister) are the laments of equals; hoy adon / hoy hodoh (ah, my lord / alas, his glory) are the laments of subjects. A king who deserves neither is stripped of all relational legitimacy.
19: kevurat chamor yiqaver sakhov vehashlelech mehal'ah sha'arei Yerushalaim (qĕbûrat ḥămôr yiqqābēr sāḥôb wĕhašlēk mĕhāʿléh šaʿărê yĕrûšālāyim): 'He will be buried like a donkey — dragged away and thrown outside the gates of Jerusalem.' Burial was essential to ancient dignity; proper burial was a mark of covenant community. To be 'buried like a donkey' means unburied, discarded as animal waste. The exact fulfillment of this prophecy is unclear — 2 Kgs 24:6 says only that 'Jehoiakim slept with his fathers,' which may be formulaic. Josephus and extra-biblical sources suggest he was killed by Nebuchadnezzar's soldiers. Whether thrown outside Jerusalem's gates or not, the oracle announces disgrace rather than honor.
20: ali halvanon utz'aqi uvbashan teni qolech (ʿălî hallĕbānôn ûṣĕʿāqî ûbabāšān tĕnî qôlēk): 'Go up to Lebanon and cry out; lift your voice in Bashan; cry from the passes of Abarim.' Jerusalem is addressed as a woman crying from the highest vantage points — Lebanon (north), Bashan (northeast, the Transjordanian plateau), and Abarim (east, the passes facing Moab from which Moses viewed the land). The three geographic extremes form a complete compass: Jerusalem's mourning cry reaches to the edges of the surrounding lands. 'All your allies have been shattered' (nishbaru kol-me'ahavayich) — the political alliances that Jerusalem trusted instead of YHWH have failed.
21: dibarti elaich bishalvotayich amarti lo eshma (dibbartî ʾēlayik bĕšalwōtāyik ʾāmartî lōʾ ʾešmāʿ): 'I warned you when you were at ease, but you said, 'I will not listen.'' The historical retrospective: YHWH's warnings came during periods of prosperity (shalvah, ease/rest) — exactly when they would least be heard. The assessment 'this has been your habit from your youth' (zeh darkech mine'uraiich) makes the refusal constitutional, not occasional. The 'youth' (ne'urim) of the nation refers to the wilderness period — the foundational era of Israelite existence was already characterized by refusal to listen (cf. Deut 9:7: 'from the day you left Egypt until you arrived here you have been rebellious').
22: kol-ro'ayich tir'eh ruach (kol-rōʿayik tirʿeh rûaḥ): 'The wind will sweep away all your rulers.' Ro'ayich (your shepherds/rulers) — the same shepherd metaphor for political leadership (cf. ch. 23, where YHWH indicts the shepherds). Ruach (wind) — the same empty-wind metaphor as 5:13 ('the prophets are nothing but wind'). The shame and disgrace of v. 22b uses the feminine suffix — Jerusalem personified as a woman whose lovers (political allies) have gone into captivity. Her wickedness (ra'atayich) is the source of her shame.
23: yoshevet baLevanon menunnah baaruzim (yōšebet ballĕbānôn mĕqunnenat bāʾărāzîm): 'You who live in Lebanon, nestled among the cedars — how you will groan when birth pangs seize you.' The birth-pain metaphor (chil keyoledet) for the pain of judgment recurs throughout Jeremiah (4:31; 6:24; 13:21; 30:6) and the prophets (Isa 13:8; 21:3; Mic 4:9-10). Nestling among the cedars — the symbol of royal luxury (the palace was built of cedar) — does not provide security. The cedar house of Lebanon, the ultimate royal fortress in imagery, will be unable to shelter its inhabitants from divine judgment.
24: chay-ani neum YHWH ki im-yihyeh Conyahu ben-Yehoyaqim melech Yehudah hotam al yad yemini (ḥay-ʾănî nĕʾum Yhwh kî ʾim-yihyeh konyāhû ben-yĕhôyāqîm melek yĕhûdāh ḥôtām ʿal yad yĕmînî): 'As surely as I live, even if Coniah were the signet ring on my right hand, I would still pull you off.' Hotam (signet ring) — the personal seal of the king, worn on the right hand, used to authenticate royal documents (cf. Esther 3:10; Hag 2:23 where YHWH reverses this judgment for Zerubbabel: 'I will make you like a signet ring'). Removing the signet ring is the removal of royal authority and identity. The self-oath (chay-ani, as I live) gives the judgment the same weight as YHWH's own existence.
25: unetaticha beyad mevaqashei nafshecha (ûnĕtattîkā bĕyad mĕbaqqĕšê napšĕkā): 'I will hand you over to those who seek your life and whom you dread.' Mevaqashei nafshecha (those who seek your life) is the covenant formula for mortal enemies who seek to kill (cf. 1 Kgs 19:10; Jer 11:21; 38:16). The specific identification — Nebuchadnezzar and the Chaldeans — names the feared enemy by name. The contrast with Hezekiah's deliverance from Sennacherib (Isa 37) is implicit: where earlier faith brought miraculous rescue, here covenant unfaithfulness brings the very enemy they feared.
26: vehitriti otcha ve'et immecha asher yeladatcha al-ha'aretz asher lo yoladtem sham: 'I will hurl you and the mother who gave birth to you into another country — a land where neither of you was born.' The hurling (hieriti, violent casting away) of both king and queen mother reflects the significance of the gevirah (queen mother) in Judean royal tradition: the mother's name is recorded alongside each king's accession (2 Kgs 24:8). Jehoiachin's exile in 597 BCE with his mother Nehushta was the historical fulfillment of this oracle (2 Kgs 24:12-15).
27: ve'el-ha'aretz asher hem menas'im et-nafshsham lashuv sham lo yashuvu: 'As for the land they long to return to — they will never return there.' The word nasa'im nafshsham (they long with all their being / lift up their soul) is the same expression as 'lifting up the soul to YHWH' in Ps 25:1; 86:4 — the most intense yearning. The refusal of return makes the exile permanent in its first announcement: no return is promised for Jehoiachin and Nehushta. Historically, Jehoiachin was eventually released from prison by Evil-merodach (2 Kgs 25:27-30) but lived in Babylon until his death, never returning to Judah.
28: ha-etsev nivzeh nefutz ha-ish hazeh Conyahu (hăʾeṣeb nibzāh nāpûṣ hāʾîš hazzeh konyāhû): 'Is this man Coniah nothing but a broken, despised clay pot — something no one wants?' The three epithets — etsev (clay vessel/idol), nivzah (despised/worthless), naphuts (broken/shattered) — progressively strip the king of dignity. The rhetorical question voices bewilderment at the magnitude of the fall: from the signet ring of YHWH's right hand (v. 24) to a broken jar no one wants. Keleh she'ein chefrtz bo (an object no one wants) — used for the worthless in Ps 22:6 and Isa 53:3 (of the Suffering Servant: 'despised and rejected').
29: eretz eretz eretz shim'i dvar YHWH (ʾereṣ ʾereṣ ʾereṣ šimĕʿî dĕbar Yhwh): 'O land, land, land, hear the word of the LORD!' The triple repetition of eretz (land/earth) is unparalleled in its forcefulness — a rhetorical triple that in the OT typically marks divine solemnity or grief (cf. Ezek 21:27: 'ruin, ruin, ruin!'; Isa 6:3: 'holy, holy, holy'). The land itself is summoned as witness — an extension of the covenant lawsuit (rib) form where heaven and earth are called as witnesses (Deut 32:1; Isa 1:2; Mic 1:2). The land must hear what the human inhabitants refused to hear.
30: kitivu et-ha'ish hazeh ariri gever lo yitzlach beyamav (kitĕbû ʾet-hāʾîš hazzeh ʿărîrî geber lōʾ yiṣlaḥ bĕyāmāyw): 'Record this man as though childless — a man who will not prosper in his lifetime; for none of his descendants will succeed in sitting on the throne of David and ruling over Judah.' Ariri (childless, literally 'stripped bare') is a legal-status term (cf. Gen 15:2; Lev 20:20-21) meaning without legitimate heirs for succession. The decree is specifically about the throne: none of Coniah's seed will yoshev al-kise David vemoshel od biYehudah (sit on the throne of David and rule in Judah). This creates the NT's genealogical problem and its solution: Matthew 1:12 includes Jeconiah (Coniah) in Jesus's legal genealogy through Joseph, while Luke 3:27 traces through a different Shealtiel and Zerubbabel. The virgin birth dissolves the curse: Jesus is legally Joseph's heir (establishing Davidic lineage) but not biologically so (bypassing the throne-curse on Jehoiachin's biological descendants). The 'childless' decree applies to throne succession through natural birth; the adoptive-legal lineage of the Son of David born of a virgin escapes it.
